risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The randomization list was generated by author (NM) using random number tables. Randomization was restricted in blocks of 10, with stratification according to gender." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation sequence using an explicit random method (random number tables), with additional block randomization and stratification, which supports low risk for random sequence generation.

Quote: "The randomization sequence was concealed centrally in sealed opaque envelopes labelled with an unique identification number. The Consultant Orthodontist at the participating site telephoned a research assistant at the co-ordinating centre to enrol a patient. The research assistant then revealed the treatment allocation by opening the next consecutive opaque envelope" Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism that prevented the recruiter from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment: central concealment with sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes opened only after enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This trial was single blind... Ideally, the clinician collecting the records at the 6-year follow-up would also have been blinded. This was not possible in this trial as the original operator would be likely to remember who had received protraction facemask treatment." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were not blinded to allocation in this orthodontic intervention, and the intervention itself (protraction facemask vs no facemask) would have been obvious. Knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence performance/cooperation and outcomes such as overjet, skeletal/occlusal changes, and psychosocial measures, so overall performance bias is high.

Quote: "This trial was single blind, as the researchers making up the panel for the orthognathic surgery decision, those measuring the radiographs and study models and the statistician were blind to the treatment/control allocation until the data were analysed. The records were labelled with a blinding ID number so the researchers did not know if the patients were in the PFG or CG." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded. The key outcomes relevant here (need for orthognathic surgery by blinded panel, cephalometric and study model measurements by blinded assessors, PAR and overjet by blinded examiners) are either judgement-based but assessed blindly or objective measurements made blinded to allocation, so lack of blinding is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "Complete records were available for 65 patients out of 73 representing an 89% attendance at 6-year follow-up." Comment: Overall attrition was 8/73 (11%), which is within a reasonable range. The loss was not large, and the authors explicitly note that they compared baseline characteristics of dropouts and completers and found no differences: "the baseline characteristics of the patients remaining in the study and the dropouts were compared... there is no attrition bias". Although the primary analysis was per-protocol and no imputation was used, the amount of missing data was modest and there is no clear evidence that dropout was imbalanced or outcome-related enough to bias the result.
